1458 Portobelo Dr, San Jose — What We Did

The seller handed me the keys.

Days 1–2: I walked the home, built the full scope of work, projected the prep budget at $18,000, projected a 16-day timeline, and collected bids from preferred vendors.

After seller approval, work started on Day 3.

13 days later this San Jose home went from needs-work to clean, move-in ready, inspected, staged, photographed, and launched.

Final prep cost: $18,454. A 2.5% variance from the original budget.

The seller never set foot in the house until it was staged and ready for market. I managed the prep, vendors, timing, updates, and decisions through shared project management in Basecamp.

This was not light prep. This was a strategic push to get the home ready without wasting the seller's time or money.

Based on comps, we estimated the $18k prep could create $100K+ in additional sale value compared with selling as-is.

The Result: The house sold at the high end of the price range for comparable semi-updated, move-in-ready homes in the neighborhood, even though the roof, major systems, repairs, and termite treatment were not done.

01

Interior Paint

  • Every surface: walls, ceilings, trim, doors, closets, baseboards, and interior details

02

Exterior Paint

  • Every surface: stucco, trim, eaves, fascia, doors, garage door, and exterior details

03

Electrical Refresh

  • 4 ceiling lights replaced
  • 26 outlets replaced
  • 14 switches replaced
  • 2 broken outlets repaired
  • 2 broken recessed lights repaired

04

Bathroom Refresh

  • Frameless shower glass added
  • Hall bathtub reglazed
  • Tile & bath areas cleaned up

05

Repairs + Cleanout

  • Fencing repaired
  • Rotted exterior wood repaired
  • Old window screens removed
  • Interior & exterior trash-out

06

Curb Appeal

  • Mulch added
  • Landscaping trimmed
  • Yard edged
  • Exterior presentation cleaned up

07

Inspections + Launch

  • Inspections completed
  • Home deep cleaned
  • Staged
  • Photographed
  • Launched to market

08

Licensed Trades

  • C-33 Painter
  • C-10 Electrician

How I Think

My golden rule

Never spend on anything buyers might rip out. Repainting cabinets, new countertops over old cabinets, renovating a bad kitchen layout, deep cleaning carpets, too-specific or trendy finishes.

Positioning

Every home can be positioned more than one way.

1458 Portobelo came across as a clear needs-work property. Walk in and you'd see damaged walls, green paint, dated gold fixtures, ceiling medallions, broken lighting, broken shower doors, older hollow-core interior doors, older door locks, handles and hinges, and updated but aging kitchens and baths.

On top of that, older systems throughout: a 25-year-old HVAC, a roof with 8 to 10 years of life left, a 100-amp panel, and windows and patio doors that didn't operate smoothly.

So where did that leave us? The neighborhood had all three tiers: as-is homes, semi-updated move-in ready homes, and fully renovated. The seller wanted a quick sale. With older systems and a galley kitchen, Portobelo wasn't going to compete with the top tier without a full 2 to 3 month renovation at a cost of over $100k. But the layout and flow of the house was above average. There was untapped potential.

So I proposed repositioning. Not from as-is to fully renovated, but from as-is to clean, move-in ready, and showing well. That's where prep dollars return the most.

Buyers didn't need to believe everything was new. They just needed to walk in and not feel like they were buying a project. Clean, cared for, easy to picture themselves living there.

What the seller said

"I didn't know where to start. Larry made the process fast, organized, and low stress.

What stood out most was how much he took off our plate. He kept the process moving, coordinated the details, and helped us get the house market-ready without it turning into a drawn-out project.

He helped us focus on the right work before going on the market, without turning it into a full renovation or pushing us to spend where we didn't need to.

When we finally saw the house after everything was finished, we were honestly amazed. We did not think it had that much potential. The whole experience felt clear, well managed, and much easier than we expected."

San Jose Seller

About Me

I bought my first home at 23 and sold it 15 months later for a 30 percent profit. That was the moment I got hooked.

Since then, for the past 23 years, I have bought, sold, renovated, and operated dozens of single family homes, small multifamily properties, condos, long term rentals, and Airbnbs. You learn differently when your own money is on the line and you're staring at your P&Ls.

That experience changed how I look at homes. I focus on the price, how buyers actually behave, the real risks, the upside and use, the layout, the true condition, and what the market is likely to reward.

My operational side came from co-founding and running a government contracting firm with 26 employees. We worked across agencies including the National Library of Medicine, USPTO, DHS CBP, and the Library of Congress. That taught me clear scope, tight budgets, vendor coordination, fast decisions, and how to get things done under pressure.

Today I bring that mix of investor judgment and operational execution to my clients. I advise sellers on what to fix, what to skip, where the risk is, and how to get the home market-ready without wasting money.

How I Work

Foundation — #1 Driver

Seller Goals

Every decision starts with what the seller actually wants: target price, speed, timeline pressure, tolerance for stress, desired net proceeds, privacy, certainty, and any personal or emotional factors.

Foundation — #2

Market Reality

Current market conditions — inventory levels, buyer demand, interest rates, competing homes, absorption rate, seasonality — heavily shape what goals are actually realistic. You can't separate this from Seller Goals.

Seller Goals + Market Reality act as the filter for everything below.

The Four Supporting Pillars

1

Positioning

How the home is presented and perceived in the marketplace. It's much more than condition — it's identifying the right buyer pool and category. Is it a lifestyle property, statement home, functional family house, entry-level starter, or investment play? All in the context of what the current market is rewarding.

2

Property Potential

Seeing the home's realistic upside — always through the lens of the current market. Study the competing homes and surrounding comps to understand the true floor and ceiling. Market reality defines what's actually achievable.

3

Risk Management

Balancing downside and upside. Spotting what could go wrong while recognizing what could go right — weighing risk vs. reward in the context of today's market. Mitigating problems when they arise, recovering quickly, and keeping all parties aligned toward a successful closing.

4

Execution

"Ideas are worthless. Execution is everything." Taking all of the above and turning them into reality — coordinating people, timelines, budgets, prep, staging, and marketing to deliver the strategy in the current market environment.
